quiz.title: Unit 5 Quiz 8, Grade 11 Chemistry
quiz.subtitle: Nomenclature and Formula Writing Practice
quiz.subject: Chemistry
quiz.topic: Naming Ionic Compounds, Transition Metals, Polyatomic Ions, Formula Writing, Stock System

questions.question: Name the compound MgC₂O₄:
questions.explanation: MgC₂O₄ is magnesium oxalate. It contains the oxalate ion (C₂O₄²⁻). Magnesium (Group 2) forms only one type of ion (+2), so no Roman numeral is needed. Magnesium carbonate (A) would be MgCO₃.

questions.question: Name the compound SnCl₂:
questions.explanation: SnCl₂ is named tin(II) chloride. Chlorine has a -1 charge, so two chlorines give -2, meaning tin has a +2 charge, indicated by (II). Tin(IV) chloride would be SnCl₄.

questions.question: What is the formula for Barium Sulfide?
questions.explanation: Barium sulfide is BaS. Barium (Group 2) forms Ba²⁺, and sulfide is S²⁻, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. BaSO₄ (C) is barium sulfate.

questions.question: Name the compound: Fe₃P₂
questions.explanation: Fe₃P₂ is named iron(II) phosphide. Phosphorus (P³⁻) has a -3 charge, so two phosphorus give -6. Therefore, three irons total +6, meaning each iron has a +2 charge, indicated by (II).

questions.question: What is the formula for Zinc Hydroxide?
questions.explanation: Zinc hydroxide is Zn(OH)₂. Zinc typically forms Zn²⁺, and hydroxide is OH⁻ with a -1 charge, so two hydroxides are needed.

questions.question: What is the formula for FrBr?
questions.explanation: FrBr is francium bromide. Francium (Group 1) forms Fr⁺, and bromide is Br⁻, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. The question asks for the formula, but the options are names. FrBr is named francium bromide.

questions.question: What is the name for CuCl?
questions.explanation: CuCl is named copper(I) chloride. Chlorine has a -1 charge, so copper must have a +1 charge, indicated by (I). Copper(II) chloride would be CuCl₂, and cupric chloride is an older name for CuCl₂.

questions.question: What is the formula for Copper(II) sulfide?
questions.explanation: Copper(II) sulfide is CuS. Copper(II) has a +2 charge (Cu²⁺), and sulfide is S²⁻ with a -2 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. Cu₂S (B) would be copper(I) sulfide.

questions.question: What is the formula for Silver(I) chloride?
questions.explanation: Silver(I) chloride is AgCl. Silver(I) has a +1 charge (Ag⁺), and chloride is Cl⁻ with a -1 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. Silver does not commonly form other charges, so the (I) is often omitted, but the formula remains AgCl.

questions.question: What is the formula for Copper(I) Oxide?
questions.explanation: Copper(I) oxide is Cu₂O. Copper(I) has a +1 charge (Cu⁺), and oxide is O²⁻, so two copper ions are needed to balance the charge.

questions.question: What is the chemical name for MgSO₃?
questions.explanation: MgSO₃ is magnesium sulfite. It contains the sulfite ion (SO₃²⁻). Magnesium sulfate (A) would be MgSO₄, and magnesium sulfide (C) would be MgS.

questions.question: What is the formula for Aluminum Chlorate?
questions.explanation: Aluminum chlorate is Al(ClO₃)₃. Aluminum forms Al³⁺, and chlorate is ClO₃⁻ with a -1 charge, so three chlorates are needed. AlCl₃ (D) would be aluminum chloride.

questions.question: What is the formula for magnesium sulfate?
questions.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is MgSO₄. Magnesium (Mg²⁺) combines with sulfate (SO₄²⁻) in a 1:1 ratio. MgS (A) is magnesium sulfide, and MgSO₃ (B) is magnesium sulfite.

questions.question: What is the formula for iron(III) phosphate?
questions.explanation: Iron(III) phosphate is FePO₄. Iron(III) has a +3 charge (Fe³⁺), and phosphate is PO₄³⁻ with a -3 charge, so they combine in a 1:1 ratio. Fe₃(PO₄)₂ (B) would be iron(II) phosphate.

questions.question: What is the formula for potassium carbonate?
questions.explanation: Potassium carbonate is K₂CO₃. Potassium (K⁺) has a +1 charge, and carbonate is CO₃²⁻ with a -2 charge, so two potassium ions are needed.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered nomenclature and formula writing! 🧪📝
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review polyatomic ion charges and transition metal oxidation states.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on distinguishing between similar ions like -ate and -ite.
scoreMessages.0: Please review naming rules for ionic compounds, including those with polyatomic ions and transition metals.
